title: In the Name of God, Go!
summary: Hosni Mubarak has become a liability for stability. Extreme wealth in the midst of extreme poverty is a shame. Now, every day that goes on, the revelations of the Mubarak family's extreme wealth in the Guardian and other media are a constant repudiation of democratic ideals in a country of (mostly poor) 80 million Egyptians. How can Egyptians achieve democracy with this living counter-example sitting before them?
body: This commentary was reposted by The Huffington Post on February 10, 2011. The latest from Belfer See more On May 7, 1940, the British Conservative MP, Leo Amery, flung these words at Neville Chamberlain during a House of Commons debate on the British-French expedition in Norway that had ended in failure. Amery repeated Oliver Cromwell's (paraphrased) words on April 20, 1653, to a Parliament attempting to remain after it had voted to dissolve itself: "You have sat too long for any good you have been doing lately. Depart, I say, and let us have done with you. In the name of God, go!" Three days later, on May 10, 1940 — the day that German armor began its thrust into the Low Countries and France — Chamberlain was replaced by the more pugnacious-looking Winston Churchill, who was to prove to be the man of Britain's "finest hour." In this recession-wracked world, where the extremes of poverty and wealth have grown to a shocking degree over the past 30 years, we learn from the Manchester Guardian (February 4, 2011) that the Mubarak family fortune could reach 70 billion dollars, with much of the wealth in British and Swiss banks or represented in real estate in London, New York, Los Angeles, and along with expensive properties around the Sharm-el-Sheikh tourist resort on the Red Sea coast. Besides the father, Hosni, the sons Gamal and Alaa are also billionaires. How dismaying it must have been to the (mostly young) demonstrators at Tahrir Square and elsewhere in Egypt to see the television clip of Hosni Mubarak on February 7 chairing his first cabinet meeting since the beginning of the uprising — giving the impression that nothing had happened in the meantime. Hosni Mubarak has become a liability for stability. Extreme wealth in the midst of extreme poverty is a shame. Now, every day that goes on, the revelations of the Mubarak family's extreme wealth in the Guardian and other media are a constant repudiation of democratic ideals in a country of (mostly poor) 80 million Egyptians. How can Egyptians achieve democracy with this living counter-example sitting before them? In the name of etc.,etc. Recommended citation Cogan, Charles G. "In the Name of God, Go!" _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article draws a historical parallel between British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain's removal in 1940 and the situation of Egyptian President Hosni Mubarak during the 2011 uprising. It recounts how MP Leo Amery used Cromwell's famous phrase 'In the name of God, go!' to demand Chamberlain's resignation following military failure, leading to Winston Churchill's appointment. The author applies this analogy to Mubarak, criticizing his perceived detachment from the Egyptian people's plight. It highlights reports of the Mubarak family's immense wealth, estimated at $70 billion, held in foreign banks and properties, contrasting sharply with widespread poverty in Egypt. The commentary argues that such extreme wealth concentration undermines democratic ideals and represents a liability for stability, questioning how true democracy can emerge while such a 'counter-example' remains in power. The piece frames Mubarak's continued rule as an obstacle to the democratic aspirations demonstrated by protesters in Tahrir Square.
result.main_content_zh: 本文通过历史类比，将1940年英国首相内维尔·张伯伦的下台与2011年埃及总统胡斯尼·穆巴拉克面临的局势相联系。文章回顾了英国议员利奥·艾默里借用克伦威尔的名言“以上帝之名，滚吧！”要求张伯伦在军事失败后辞职，从而促成了温斯顿·丘吉尔的上台。作者将此类比应用于穆巴拉克，批评其与埃及人民疾苦的脱节。文章重点引用了关于穆巴拉克家族巨额财富的报道，估计达700亿美元，存放于外国银行及房产中，与埃及普遍的贫困形成鲜明对比。评论认为，这种极端的财富集中破坏了民主理想，并对稳定构成威胁，质疑在这样的“反面榜样”持续掌权的情况下，真正的民主如何能够实现。文章将穆巴拉克的持续统治描绘成塔希尔广场抗议者所展现的民主诉求的障碍。
